request.question: Which tower was funded through the EB-5 visa program, the Wall Street Tower or the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower?

title: Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower
Protective Life
Arrivé
Hedden Construction Company
EB-5 visa
Flatiron Building
Wall Street Tower
Lyceum Theatre (Park Avenue South)
Physicians Mutual
Abbas Hashmi
sentences: The Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower, colloquially known as the Met Life Tower, is a landmark skyscraper located on Madison Avenue near the intersection with East 23rd Street, across from Madison Square Park in Manhattan, New York City.
Designed by the architectural firm of Napoleon LeBrun & Sons and built by the Hedden Construction Company, the tower is modeled after the Campanile in Venice, Italy.

The hotel located in the clock tower portion of the building has the address 5 Madison Avenue, while the office building covering the rest of the block, occupied primarily by Credit Suisse, is referred to as 1 Madison Avenue.
Protective Life Corporation is a financial service holding company in Birmingham, Alabama.
The company’s primary subsidiary, Protective Life Insurance Company, was established in 1907 and now markets its products and services in all 50 states.
As of December 31, 2016, the corporation had more than 2,700 employees, annual revenues of $4.48 billion and assets of $75 billion.

In addition to Protective Life Insurance Company, Protective Life Corporation's subsidiaries include West Coast Life Insurance Company, MONY Life Insurance Company, Protective Life And Annuity Insurance Company, ProEquities Inc./Protective Securities, and Lyndon Property Insurance Company.
Arrivé ( ) is a 440 ft , 41-story skyscraper in the Belltown neighborhood of Seattle, Washington, that began construction in April 2015.

The $190 million project, originally named Potala Tower after the Potala Palace in Tibet, was designed by Weber Thompson and consists of 342 apartments and a 142-room hotel operated under the Hotel Indigo brand.
It was financed partially by Chinese nationals through the EB-5 visa program and is scheduled to be completed in January 2019.

Some of the finest buildings in New Jersey, New York City, and other large eastern cities were built by the Hedden Construction Company, one of the largest construction companies operating in Newark in the very early 1900s.
Among the most notable is the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower located at One Madison Avenue in New York, NY.
The tower was the world's tallest building from 1909 to 1913 and home to the Hedden Construction Company's main offices located on the 36th and 37th floors.
During this prosperous period over $40,000,000 in construction contracts and payments were collected by the firm.

The EB-5 visa, "employment-based fifth preference category" or "EB-5 Immigrant Investor Visa Program", created in 1990 by the Immigration Act of 1990, provides a method for eligible Immigrant Investors to become lawful permanent residents — informally known as "green card" holders — by investing at least $1,000,000 to finance a business in the United States that will employ at least 10 American workers."
Most immigrant investors who use the EB-5 program invest in a targeted employment area (TEA) — a rural area or area with high unemployment — which lowers the investment threshold to $500,000.
The EB-5 program is intended to encourage both "foreign investments and economic growth."
The EB-5 Immigrant Investor Visa Program is one of five employment-based (EB) preference programs in the United States.

The Flatiron Building, originally the Fuller Building, is a triangular 22-story steel-framed landmarked building located at 175 Fifth Avenue in the borough of Manhattan, New York City, and is considered to be a groundbreaking skyscraper.

Upon completion in 1902, it was one of the tallest buildings in the city at 20 floors high and one of only two skyscrapers north of 14th Street – the other being the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower, one block east.
The building sits on a triangular block formed by Fifth Avenue, Broadway, and East 22nd Street, with 23rd Street grazing the triangle's northern (uptown) peak.
As with numerous other wedge-shaped buildings, the name "Flatiron" derives from its resemblance to a cast-iron clothes iron.
Wall Street Tower is a mixed-use building under development in the Financial District of Manhattan.
The building is being developed by Lightstone Group and was designed by British architect David Adjaye.
The building will be funded through the EB-5 visa program and through more traditional sources of capital.
The Lyceum Theatre was a theatre in New York City located on Fourth Avenue, now Park Avenue South, between 23rd and 24th Streets in Manhattan.
It was built in 1885 and operated until 1902, when it was torn down to make way for the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower.
It was replaced by a new Lyceum Theatre on 45th Street.
For most of its existence, the theatre was home to Daniel Frohman’s Lyceum Theatre Stock Company, which presented many important plays and actors of the day.
Physicians Mutual is a privately held insurance company headquartered in Omaha, Nebraska, United States, that consists of Physicians Mutual Insurance Company and Physicians Life Insurance Company.
Founded as Physicians Mutual Insurance Company in 1902 by Edwin E. Elliott, Physicians Mutual began by selling health insurance to medical professionals.
Policies were offered to the general public starting in 1962, and by 1970 the company expanded into life insurance when it founded Physicians Life Insurance Company.
Today the company offers a variety of insurance products, annuities, Medicare, Medigap, Medicare Supplement, Term Life Insurance, Whole Life Insurance, Cancer and funeral pre-planning services.
It holds over US$3 billion in assets and employs over one thousand people.
Robert A. Reed is chief executive officer and president.
Abbas Hashmi is an investment banker most well known for founding Green Card Capital.
Hashmi is an industry specialist and supports the EB-5 visa program and the use of transparency and compliance to run the program.
He works with investors, foreign nationals, and high net worth individuals and their families with the EB-5 visa program.
Hashmi has worked for AIG as regional head for the Middle East region.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Which tower was funded through the EB-5 visa program, the Wall Street Tower or the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower?
hop1_docs: Wall Street Tower | Wall Street Tower is a mixed-use building under development in the Financial District of Manhattan.  The building is being developed by Lightstone Group and was designed by British architect David Adjaye.  The building will be funded through the EB-5 visa program and through more traditional sources of capital.

Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower | The Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower, colloquially known as the Met Life Tower, is a landmark skyscraper located on Madison Avenue near the intersection with East 23rd Street, across from Madison Square Park in Manhattan, New York City.  Designed by the architectural firm of Napoleon LeBrun & Sons and built by the Hedden Construction Company, the tower is modeled after the Campanile in Venice, Italy.  The hotel located in the clock tower portion of the building has the address 5 Madison Avenue, while the office building covering the rest of the block, occupied primarily by Credit Suisse, is referred to as 1 Madison Avenue.

100 Montgomery Street | 100 Montgomery Street also known as the Equitable Life Building is an office tower located in the financial district of San Francisco, California.  The 354 ft , 25-floor tower was completed in 1955 and served as headquarters to the Equitable Life Insurance Company.

AXA Towers | The AXA Towers are two commercial office buildings in Syracuse, New York.  AXA Tower I was built in 1966 and Tower II was built in 1971, and both stand at 81.7 meters and have 19 floors.  They are tied for the second tallest buildings in Syracuse.  They were both built with a tube-in-tube structural system with a steel and glass curtain wall facade.  The buildings serve as offices for New-York-based AXA Equitable Life Insurance Company, and have illuminated logos on top.

Morden Tower | The Morden Tower in Back Stowell Street on the West Walls of Newcastle upon Tyne, England, is a Scheduled Ancient Monument and a Grade 1 listed building.  Since June 1964, Connie Pickard has been custodian of Morden Tower, and has made it a key fixture of Newcastle's alternative cultural life, with the building hosting music and poetry events often funded by Pickard.

Double Tower | The Double Tower is one of the 17 towers which were part of the city walls of Waterford, Munster, Ireland.  It was built in the latter part of the 15th century and the early part of the 16th century.  Six of the original 17 towers survive to this day; the other five being Reginald's Tower, Beach Tower, Semi-Lunar Tower, French Tower, and Watch Tower (Waterford).  The Double Tower is located on Castle Street, between the French Tower and the Watch Tower.  All three towers are well-preserved, along with some parts of the old city wall between them.
Tower Insurance | TOWER Insurance is a New Zealand-based insurance company which provides fire & general insurance.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Wall Street Tower: Passage [1] ("Wall Street Tower") contains the explicit token "funded through the EB-5 visa program" (quote: "funded through the EB-5 visa program") and also notes developer/designer ("Lightstone Group", "David Adjaye") — see passage [1].
  - Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower: Passage [2] ("Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower") includes the name and colloquial form "Met Life Tower" (quote: "colloquially known as the Met Life Tower") but contains no EB-5 funding token in the provided excerpt — see passage [2].
- Ambiguities:
  - The Met Life entry appears under two labels/variants in the passage: "Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower" and "Met Life Tower". No alternate candidates for "Wall Street Tower" appear in the passages.
  - The provided Met Life excerpt lacks any funding/development funding detail (so absence of EB-5 mention here is not definitive).
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as named in the passages): "Wall Street Tower".
  - Precise missing fact needed: explicit funding-status statement for "Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower" (i.e., whether that tower was funded via the EB-5 visa program).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open the "Wall Street Tower" page — check the lede/infobox and the "Development" / "Funding" / "History" sections to confirm the EB-5 funding statement and any citations for that claim. Search keywords: "Wall Street Tower EB-5 funding", "Wall Street Tower Lightstone EB-5".
  2) Open the "Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower" (a.k.a. "Met Life Tower") page — check the lede/infobox and "History" / "Development" / "Funding" sections for any mention of EB-5 funding or development financing. Search keywords: "Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower EB-5", "Met Life Tower funding".
  3) If the Met Life page lacks funding details, fetch related articles or news sources linked from that page (references) or search for "Met Life Tower financing" and "EB-5" to confirm absence/presence.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and inspected.
hop2_docs: Wall Street Tower | Wall Street Tower is a mixed-use building under development in the Financial District of Manhattan.  The building is being developed by Lightstone Group and was designed by British architect David Adjaye.  The building will be funded through the EB-5 visa program and through more traditional sources of capital.

Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower | The Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower, colloquially known as the Met Life Tower, is a landmark skyscraper located on Madison Avenue near the intersection with East 23rd Street, across from Madison Square Park in Manhattan, New York City.  Designed by the architectural firm of Napoleon LeBrun & Sons and built by the Hedden Construction Company, the tower is modeled after the Campanile in Venice, Italy.  The hotel located in the clock tower portion of the building has the address 5 Madison Avenue, while the office building covering the rest of the block, occupied primarily by Credit Suisse, is referred to as 1 Madison Avenue.

Morden Tower | The Morden Tower in Back Stowell Street on the West Walls of Newcastle upon Tyne, England, is a Scheduled Ancient Monument and a Grade 1 listed building.  Since June 1964, Connie Pickard has been custodian of Morden Tower, and has made it a key fixture of Newcastle's alternative cultural life, with the building hosting music and poetry events often funded by Pickard.

Panorama Tower | Panorama Tower, previously known as 1101 Brickell, is a mixed-use 85 story skyscraper under construction in Miami, Florida, United States.  It is located in the Brickell district of Downtown Miami.  It was originally approved by the City of Miami and the Federal Aviation Administration in 2006 but was put on hold due to the Great Recession.  The project was revived in 2012 when owner Tibor Hollo hired Moshe Cosicher, AIA Architect, to redesign the project.  In 2013, the project was revised; the 868 ft tower is stated to include residential, hotel, retail, and office space.  When completed, it will be the tallest building in Miami, but may soon thereafter be overtaken by various other towers, including two of their own projects (The Towers by Foster + Partners and One Bayfront Plaza).  It will take over the Four Seasons Hotel Miami as tallest when it tops out in 2017.  The project is the first development in the City of Miami to be funded in part by EB-5 visas.  While the entire site was technically addressed 1101 Brickell Avenue, the building will actually be located on the back of the lot, behind two existing office buildings, at 1100 Brickell Bay Drive.  It will be about two blocks from the Tenth Street Metromover station.

BT Financial Group | BT Financial Group is a financial services company based at Tower Two, International Towers Sydney in Sydney, Australia, with operations there and in New Zealand.  Its product range includes managed investments, life insurance, superannuation and discount brokering products.  BT also offers custody and settlement services to institutional customers and fund managers.

Wardenclyffe Tower | Wardenclyffe Tower (1901–1917), also known as the Tesla Tower, was an early wireless transmission station designed and built by Nikola Tesla in Shoreham, New York in 1901-1902.  Tesla intended to transmit messages, telephony and even facsimile images across the Atlantic to England and to ships at sea based on his theories of using the Earth to conduct the signals.  His decision to scale up the facility and add his ideas of wireless power transmission to better compete with Guglielmo Marconi's radio based telegraph system was met with the project's primary backer, financier J. P. Morgan, refusing to fund the changes.  Additional investment could not be found and the project was abandoned in 1906 and never became operational.

Jonny Kennedy | Jonny Kennedy (4 November 1966 – 26 September 2003) was the subject of the documentary "The Boy Whose Skin Fell Off", which documented the final months of his life as he suffered from the rare inherited condition known as dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa (EB or DEB).  He was born with no skin on his left leg.  Kennedy ultimately died of skin cancer caused by EB.
response.summary_2: Wall Street Tower was funded through the EB-5 visa program. Wall Street Tower is a mixed-use building in the Financial District of Manhattan developed by Lightstone Group and designed by British architect David Adjaye. Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower, colloquially known as the Met Life Tower, is a landmark skyscraper on Madison Avenue near Madison Square Park.
